No rule sanctions conversational filler, restatement, or low-density connective tissue. The model is correctly executing what it's told; what it's told has no slack.

top_root_causes.cause: Uniform segment template — same internal arc every time
top_root_causes.evidence: listicle.md beat 4 prescribes 'reframe -> mechanism -> hard rule' for EVERY item-section. The model executes mechanically: every segment in perennials_v1 opens with a stencil ('Confess something' / 'Here's the one I promised you' / 'Most beginners get hostas exactly backwards' / 'What if I told you' / 'This is the big one'), ends with a hard rule under 10 words, and bridges with a teaser. Successful roses uses 10 segments with much more variation — the 'I promised you' callback appears ONCE.
top_root_causes.why: The listicle module's beat-by-beat arc applied to every item produces visible uniformity. The opener-variety rule prevents repeating the same opener but still forces an opener-as-rhetorical-move on every section — successful scripts often just start flatly. Successful writers vary segment SHAPE as well as content.

top_root_causes.cause: Aphoristic closer enforced as mandatory, not optional
top_root_causes.evidence: listicle.md beat 6c ('Hard rule before you leave') and beat 8 ('Aphorism button') BOTH require quotable lines at every segment end. Every perennials_v1 segment ends with one: 'Feet moist. Collar dry.' / 'Restraint now. Spears forever.' / 'Bury the shovel.' / 'Pick well. Bury the shovel.' / 'Right plant. Right seat. Non-negotiable.' Successful lavender Secret 5 ends with a flowing sentence — no aphorism. The repeated 4-word-verdict pattern is the single loudest LLM tell.
top_root_causes.why: Two rules push the same direction (hard rule + aphorism button) and both are mandatory per segment. The result is metronomic — every segment lands on the same percussive shape, and the device loses power through repetition. Successful writers use aphoristic closers sparingly, maybe twice per script.

top_root_causes.cause: Mechanism explained as personification rather than biology
top_root_causes.evidence: Successful hydrangeas: 'Acidic soil (around pH 4.5 to 5.5) makes aluminum more available to the plant, which drives the flowers blue.' Names chemical agent, gating variable, directional cause-effect. Generated perennials: 'the soil insulates them from winter chill, and the plant decides this year isn't worth the show.' Personifies ('decides') and skips actual cold-requirement-for-bud-set biology.
top_root_causes.why: The 'Mechanism over citation' rule says explain WHY in physical/biological/causal terms but doesn't ban load-bearing personification ('the plant decides') as the mechanism itself. The gardening channel's 'Plant as character with a survival agenda' rule actively encourages this. The two rules collide: character-personification fills the mechanism slot.

top_root_causes.cause: Central metaphor over-extended across whole script
top_root_causes.evidence: Perennials hammers 'pension in the ground' across hook, segment 1, segment 2, conclusion ('pay you back for years,' 'savings plan,' 'a savings account that hasn't been funded yet,' 'Eat for two decades,' 'Collect the pension'). Successful lavender uses 'tough Mediterranean weed wearing a pretty dress' ONCE. The Echo phrase rule in gardening.md actively MANDATES 2-3 mid-body restatements plus a conclusion landing.
top_root_causes.why: The gardening channel's 'Echo phrase — metaphor-family exclusive' rule REQUIRES 2-3 restatements plus conclusion landing. The model correctly executes a mandatory rule, but the rule itself produces 'monolithic metaphor' — a writing exercise rather than knowledge.

top_root_causes.evidence: Generated drops 'tetraploid,' 'Hemerocallis fulva,' 'lignify before frost,' 'remontant,' 'doubled chromosome set' into beginner copy. The 'sandpaper sound at dusk' detail is too cinematic and too cleverly tied to the technical point. Successful lavender drops Latin only when distinguishing varieties the viewer will shop for ('Munstead and Hidcote').
top_root_causes.why: 'Domain vocabulary is your credential' (gardening.md) plus 'Translate jargon in the same breath' (script_base) together encourage MORE jargon-drops because the model is rewarded for using them properly. Missing: a rule limiting jargon to terms the viewer needs to shop or act on.

proposed_interventions.intervention: Add a 'breathing-room' rule mandating low-density beats after high-density ones: 'After a fragment chain, metaphor reveal, or mechanism punch, the next 1-2 sentences must be deliberately low-density — conversational connective tissue, a restatement in plainer words, or a friendly aside. Conversational filler like Here's the thing, Now let's talk about, Let me sell you on, Are you ready, I know that sounds insane, One more thing on X is REQUIRED at least 3x per script and is not weakness — it is pacing.'
proposed_interventions.rationale: Single missing counterweight to the density rules. Every other rule pushes toward maximum information-per-sentence; none sanction slack. Adding an explicit slack quota lets the model produce dynamic range rather than flat-peak prose. Addresses density maximization and bleeds into voice naturalness, pacing, and LLM-tells simultaneously.
proposed_interventions.risk: May cause mechanical insertion of filler phrases, creating new tells ('Here's the thing' becomes a new stencil). Mitigate by giving 4-5 example shapes and requiring variety. Slightly increases word count.

proposed_interventions.intervention: Soften listicle.md beat 6c+8 from mandatory to budgeted. Replace 'Hard rule before you leave' + 'Aphorism button' (both required per segment) with: 'Across the whole script, deploy 2-3 aphoristic closers MAXIMUM, reserved for the highest-stakes segments. Every other segment must close with either a flowing sentence, a story-image, or a clean handoff — NOT a 3-7 word verdict. The repeated 4-word-verdict pattern is the single loudest LLM tell.'
proposed_interventions.rationale: The uniformity of segment closers is the most identifiable LLM tell, caused by a mandatory rule. Converting from per-segment requirement to per-script budget directly removes the metronomic feel.
proposed_interventions.risk: Model may under-deliver on memorable lines entirely. Mitigate by keeping the rule that the 2-3 used aphorisms must be earned. User may value aphoristic closers as brand signature.

proposed_interventions.intervention: Add an 'uneven segment shape' rule: 'At least 30% of segments must end CLEANLY — no aphorism, no thematic-bridge teaser, just stop and start the next segment with a fresh dare or framing. Forbid the X. The next Y. transition shape from appearing in more than 50% of seams.'
proposed_interventions.rationale: Addresses the 'tour bus announcing the next stop' problem. The current 'Transitions are open loops' rule doesn't cap how OFTEN the open-loop transition appears, so the model uses it on every seam.
proposed_interventions.risk: May produce abrupt seams. Mitigate by tying it to importance: 'leave flat the supporting/low-stakes segments; engineer bridges only for peak segments.'

proposed_interventions.intervention: Ban load-bearing personification as mechanism. Add to script_base 'Mechanism over citation' section: 'Personification as COLOR is welcome (the plant sulks, the roots chase water) but personification as MECHANISM is banned. If the explanation hinges on a verb like decides, wants, panics, knows — the next sentence must give the actual physical/biological/chemical agent. Example BAD: the plant decides this year isn't worth the show. Example GOOD: the plant decides this year isn't worth the show — the dormancy hormones that trigger bud-set need real surface chill, and buried eyes never feel it.'
proposed_interventions.rationale: The gardening channel's 'Plant as character' rule fights the 'Mechanism IS the proof' rule. This intervention resolves the collision: personification stays as flavor but never substitutes for biology. Addresses uneven explanation quality (great once or twice, weak elsewhere).
proposed_interventions.risk: May push the model toward over-explaining mechanism in segments where the rule isn't needed. Real biology may not always be in research input — the rule needs to fail gracefully ('if mechanism isn't in research, cut the claim entirely').

proposed_interventions.intervention: Demote the echo-phrase rule from MANDATORY restatement to OPTIONAL. Change gardening.md from 'One signature image, installed early, 2-3 mid-body restatements, 1 landing in conclusion — max 4 placements total' to 'AT MOST one signature image, used AT MOST twice in the script. Local, disposable metaphors per segment are the default. The pension-in-the-ground / savings-plan / insurance-policy pattern across an entire script reads as a writing exercise.'
proposed_interventions.rationale: The mandatory 2-3 restatements directly produces the 'monolithic metaphor' tell. Successful lavender uses its central metaphor ONCE. Cohesion is achieved via the running antagonist and master question, not via metaphor repetition.
proposed_interventions.risk: May make scripts feel less unified or less brand-distinct. User may value the echo phrase as a craft signal. Test on a single script first; reversible if it reads as scattered.

proposed_interventions.intervention: Cap jargon to action-relevant terms only. Add to gardening.md 'Domain vocabulary': 'Latin binomials, cultivar names, and technical terms (tetraploid, lignify, anaerobic, remontant) appear ONLY when the viewer needs them to shop, identify, or act. Decorative jargon — biology terms dropped to signal expertise without changing what the viewer does — is banned. Test: if the term were removed, would the viewer's behavior change? If no, cut it.'
proposed_interventions.rationale: Directly addresses the credential-flexing LLM tell. Successful scripts pass this test naturally ('Munstead and Hidcote' tells you what to buy); generated fails it ('doubled chromosome set' in a daylily aside does nothing for the viewer).
proposed_interventions.risk: Low. The model may slightly under-use vocabulary that does pass the test, but 'Domain vocabulary is your credential' still operates.

synthesis.structural_observation: The gap is real but narrowly located: it's a VOICE NATURALNESS and PACING issue, not a structure, substance, or craft issue. The same-topic hydrangeas comparison is the most honest data point in the bundle — sentence-for-sentence, generated is the better-written script. What still feels off is not the writing quality but the writing UNIFORMITY: every segment hits the same beats with the same density at the same tempo, because the prompt aggressively mandates density on every sentence and provides zero rules sanctioning slack, restatement, or flat connective tissue. This is not a model-ceiling issue and not unfixable — it's a rule-balance issue. The current prompt teaches everything a great script should HAVE (earned sentences, mechanism, fragments, vivid verbs, aphorisms, metaphors, callbacks) but nothing about what a great script ALLOWS ITSELF TO LACK in places (boring transitions, restating the same idea twice, a segment that ends flatly). The single highest-leverage move is adding a breathing-room counterweight; the second is demoting the per-segment aphorism mandate to a per-script budget. After those two changes, the remaining gap is largely aesthetic preference about register (sage vs friend), which is fixable but lower-leverage.
what_generated_does_BETTER_than_successf: Stronger, more cinematic hooks with specific second-person sensory failure imagery (sagging shrub, crispy tan edges, leaves like wet flags)

Sharper sensory specifics inside instructions (tuna can = 1 inch, soil clumps like the edge of a brownie, two knuckles deep, no volcanoes, 2pm hairdryer test) — repeatedly flagged as 'screenshot-worthy'
Novel segment topics competitors don't cover (winter burlap cage for hydrangea bud protection, species-ID walk-outside diagnostic)
Tighter per-segment retention bridges that explicitly forward-reference the next segment's stake
More biologically substantiated explanation in the few places where it lands (macrophylla transpiration / leaf thickness differential)
Identity-payoff conclusions with stronger emotional resonance ('you're the gardener who builds a forest floor under it, not the one who trusts a plastic tag')
Higher density of named cultivars and per-species prescriptions when those serve the viewer's shopping decision
next_question_for_user: Strategic ordering: do you want the prompt to also drive OUTLINE-level decisions (which topics to cover, what to lead with)? The hydrangeas comparison shows generated had the wrong topic selection and lead-order — an outline problem, not a script problem, that the script prompt cannot fix.

Voice register target: do you want the channel to sound like a 'friend across the fence' (successful lavender/roses register — folksy, peer-level, kitchen metaphors) or are you fine with the current 'clever essayist with parables' register that the generated scripts produce? Both can work for gardening on YouTube; the fix path is very different depending on which you want.

Aphorism appetite: the 4-word verdict closers ('Feet moist. Collar dry.') are the loudest LLM tell, but they're also memorable and brandable. Are they an asset you want to keep (dialed down to 2-3 per script for impact), or a tell you want to mostly eliminate?

Audience accessibility vs. prose craft: generated runs ~1,950-2,150 words against successful ~1,850-2,600 with much higher per-sentence cognitive load. For beginners watching while folding laundry, accessibility matters more; for a sophisticated gardening audience, craft wins. Who is this channel actually for?
